author: @edzammit9050
text: Ball point pen can be used for making a uni pivot tonearm for a turntable. Ive made a tonearm with a pen as a center bearing and there is no friction at all. The tonearm will spin freely for 5 mins!! The are plenty of diy websites showing how its done. A Canadian guy who goes by the name of nanook made a tonearm for $2.19 and its now known as nanook219 tonearm. Its suppose to sound amazing. As good as hi end tonearms   😊

author: @wirebrushproductions1001
text: Your ballpoint thrust bearing won't last long. Ballpoint balls are engineered to grab almost anything - if it doesn't spin, it won't draw ink to the paper. You've washed out the ink and given it a spray of silicone, so that's your lubricant. When that gets used up, the ballpoint balls will show increased drag on the bottom of the wells they sit in. So your next challenge will be to design a lubricant well for the ballpoints. Real ball bearings work because no surface slides on any other surface, but that's no what's going on in your design.

The level of imagination in these videos is nothing short of inspiring.
I like to think “everything can be a tool (or a part for some structure or mechanism, or even art...)” and here is this gentleman proving the point every single time. The bit of string to start the flywheel gave me a good chuckle.

author: @stuffoflardohfortheloveof
text: Slightly off topic - would increased weight ever defeat a magnetic bearing? or can a magnetic field never be entirely destroyed?

author: @jonbutcher9805
text:
Mr Robert. Nothing to do with bearings. But as you we're putting it all together you off handedly said here's a ring with magnets and it suddenly hit me. Why not make an episode or episodes of making a permanent magnets using DC voltage and the metals, ceramics and rubbers instead of buying magnets.
Not a project for everyone for sure, but I believe there would be enough interest in the basics of magnet making. A myriad variety of ways and means and I believe a useful skill to aquire in the on going fascination with all things power generation.
Just a quick thought for a fascinating subject matter should you find yourself looking for future ideas and projects.
If you've already covered this subject apologies for wasting your time.
Keep up the great works. You are a treasure trove of knowledge and inspiration for so many.

author: @ianbottom7396
text: Robert have you considered using jewel bearings? Eons ago as an apprentice in the meter and test section we had to refurbish old school electricity kWh meters and the bearings at the ends of the shaft of the flat disc rotor were jewel bearings that installed from a dispenser that released them one at a time into a cup arrangement on the end of the shaft. I doubt you will find any current designs of meters using a rotating disc anymore but the jewel bearings must be still used by watch and clock makers

author: @salimufari
text: I can see taking that pen nib assembly inverting a second one & attaching that to the drive shaft with the large ball as the interface but totally loose. That way the large ball can be free to move avoiding a wear ring from being stationary. Not sure the gain is worth the trouble but it could extend the useful life.

author: @MichaelKunz-mt2oo
text: I like the home made nib pen from a soda can. When I was in elementary school teachers still used inkwells and dipped their pens. Our wooden desks all had uniform cutouts for the ink wells.

replies.author: @edwardphillips8460
replies.text: I know those desks well. My mum took me to see her 1 room school house back in rural Ontario. That the desk exactly.

replies.author: @monadyne
replies.text: "Our wooden desks all had uniform cutouts for the ink wells." Although dip pens and actual ink wells were gone by the time I started school in the 1950s, we still had those desks. Battle-scarred after decades of abuse by kids, they were still strong and eminently serviceable. Things were so well-built back then!

replies.author: @paulfrost8952
replies.text: We had those desks when I was at school circa 1975 they were filled with chewing gum and paper though 😂

replies.author: @MichaelKunz-mt2oo
replies.text: @paulfrost8952 Those wooden desks lasted forever. At some point towards the end of my high school years we received our first of the plastic and steel combo desk/chairs. Some of them were trash before the end of their first year.

replies.author: @paulfrost8952
replies.text: @MichaelKunz-mt2oo that doesn’t surprise me. We stared getting those in the mid 70’s, but the wooden ones had graffiti that was at least 20years old and would still be serviceable had they not been replaced by flimsy metal and plastic desks with side hinged desk top.

author: @em9594
text: You should of emptied the ink out of the pens and refilled them with light weight oil, so you'll have an auto oiling micro bearing. Remember to push a bit of plastic or rubber up into the oil filled pen cartridge to pressurize the oil so it'll dispense onto the bearings as they turn

replies.author: @jimlipscomb3236
replies.text: In the video he says he drained the pens then sprayed them with with silicone lubricant. It occurred to me to fill the pens with lubricant as well.

replies.author: @TheModerateNewb
replies.text: I commented a step further just now to allow a gravity fed lubricant delivery based on this very concept you bring up. Thanks for the inspiration

replies.author: @mafftv3801
replies.text: He hasn’t used the barrels to the pens so there is no reservoir to hold the oil

replies.author: @TheModerateNewb
